design_philosophy.core_loop: Manipulate planetary parameters (atmosphere, temperature, landmasses) to guide life evolution from single-celled organisms through sentient civilizations to eventual exodus, observing emergent ecological outcomes across ~10 billion simulated years
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing competing planetary systems—maintaining habitable temperatures, atmospheric composition, and ecosystem stability while managing player-triggered interventions and natural disasters; tension between player agency and emergent complexity
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: God-like planetary architect observing and steering the deep-time evolution of worlds; scientific sandbox exploration of life's potential forms and civilizational outcomes
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Incorporates James Lovelock's Gaia hypothesis as core mechanical framework; embraces scientific complexity and emergent unpredictability (species may thrive or die 'for no apparent reason'); rejects traditional win/lose conditions in favor of open-ended observation and experimentation; treats all animal taxa as equally viable evolutionary paths (sapient molluscs possible; no mammalian bias)

difficulty.checkpointing: Planet development stages can be restored and repeated; planets persist for ~10 billion simulated years until death via solar expansion
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Selectable difficulty levels modulate energy unit budget (lowest level provides unlimited budget); Random Planet mode is software toy without required goals; eight Scenario modes impose specific objectives with varying challenges (terraforming, civilization rescue)

progression.upgrade_system: Planetary evolution stages tied to life complexity: single-celled → multicellular → sentient → civilizational development (Aquarium, Medieval, Industrial, Nuclear, Nano-tech ages referenced implicitly); civilization technology levels determine energy budget and disaster vulnerability
progression.unlock_mechanism: Eight scenario modes unlock conceptually: three Earth history stages (Aquarium, Cambrian, 1990), four terraforming challenges (Mars, Venus, Ice Planet, Dune), one post-apocalyptic rescue scenario (Earth 2XXX); Daisy World special biome mode; machine life Easter egg unlocks via nanotech city destruction
replayability_hooks: Random Planet mode generates infinite procedural challenges with no set goals
Multiple scenario objectives with different planetary starting conditions
Experimentation with evolutionary branches—species extinction followed by diversification enables new ecological experiments
Sandbox manipulation: place equipment (oxygen generators, monolith for intelligence enhancement) to test causal interventions
Disaster response strategies vary by civilization stage and resource availability

world_building_techniques: Gaia hypothesis scientific framework (James Lovelock as advisor/manual introduction) grounds planetary mechanics in real planetary science philosophy
Evolutionary timescale framing (10 billion year lifespan matching solar evolution) provides narrative context for urgency of sentient life development
Scenario-based historical storytelling: Aquarium/Cambrian/1990 Earth stages tell Earth's own developmental story; terraforming scenarios imply multi-world colonization narrative
Disaster chains create implicit cause-effect narratives (volcanic eruptions → atmospheric dust → temperature drop → ecosystem stress)
2001: A Space Odyssey monolith Easter egg injects sci-fi narrative texture
Civilization exodus condition (ultimate goal) implies implicit narrative of species maturation and departure

faction_indicators: Civilization technology ages imply societal development stages with distinct capabilities
Multiple sapient species on same planet create implicit competition/cooperation dynamics (though game mechanics for inter-species conflict undocumented)
Nuclear war triggered by fuel shortage suggests civilization-level resource conflict
danger_signals: Global warming with cascading consequences: ice cap melt → sea level rise → ocean boiling → permanent desertification
Nuclear winter from warfare or eruptions creates civilizational collapse conditions
Mass extinction events reset evolutionary landscape, potentially destroying player-invested species
Disease and pollution scale with population, creating population control dilemmas
Oxygen/atmospheric imbalance can make planets uninhabitable for multicellular life
environmental_storytelling: Atmospheric composition displays reveal invisible planetary chemistry changes
Continental drift visualization shows deep-time geological transformation
Species mutation rates and reproduction controls expose players to evolutionary mechanisms
Disaster frequency and severity tied to development level: more advanced civilizations vulnerable to nuclear/pollution cascades
Barren planets (no water, extreme temperatures) tell ecological failure stories; recovery via comet strikes offers redemption arc
